Name: 28.11 Oregon Prison Sagebrush Project
Description: In 2010, “Oregon Field Guide” looked at an innovative project in Washington state focusing on sustainability programs in prison. The project has since expanded to Oregon and other western states. In Oregon, the Institute for Applied Ecology in Corvallis is working with the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) and the Oregon Department of Corrections to create a massive sagebrush growing program to rehabilitate land after wildfires and restore habitat for sage grouse. “Oregon Field Guide” followed prisoners at the Snake River Correctional Institution as they grew and transplanted specially selected sagebrush seeds near Vale. These efforts kicked off a curated western sagebrush seed nursery designed to assist the BLM with future restoration efforts in eastern Oregon, as well as engage prisoners in ecology-based education and work.

Name: 28.12 Remembering Jeff Douglas
Description: "Oregon Field Guide" lost a dear friend in February 2017. Jeff Douglas was one of the original "Field Guide" show producers from 1990 to 2000. Jeff was a lover of rivers and ranches and he gave voice to people of every background across the state. To those of us that worked with him, though, he’ll be remembered as a friend and a truly great guy first and foremost. We're feeling his loss deeply and as you can see through his stories and his amazing contributions over the years, Oregon just lost a pal as well.

Description: Have you ever wondered how OPB manages to transmit its signal all across 94,000 square miles of our big state? Thank our field engineers, who like the intrepid letter carriers, brave snow, wind, ice, scorning sun and everything in between to maintain the 82 high towers and other mountain-top hardware that carry OPB’s radio and TV signals through the air.

Description: There are no cows left on Pine Creek Conservation Area, northeast of Bend. But there is barbed wire fence. Soon part of the conservation area will become federally protected wilderness, and backcountry volunteers are racing against that deadline to remove the derelict fence from the land — before wilderness restrictions make an arduous task even more difficult.

Name: 29.02 Search for Oregon's Tallest Trees
Description: As LiDAR technology maps more and more of our state, DOGAMI scientist Ian Madin is finding tall trees no one knew about before. In this this story, Ian brings champion tree climbers Brian French and Will Koomjian (Ascending the Giants) up to date with his latest findings. Once identified, the Ascending the Giants team leaves the fancy technology behind and heads out into the forest to "proof" these measurements by actually climbing the trees and dropping a measuring tape.

Framing the swirling drips in colorful close-up, Kevin hopes to bring greater public awareness to oil pollution.

Name: 29.04 Climate Change Butterflies
Description: Sea-level rise is expected to have wide-ranging effects on Pacific Northwest, and one of the region’s rarest butterfly species is in trouble. In 2008, a storm surge wiped out the strongest remaining colony of San Juan Island’s dwindling Island Marble butterfly population. Now, researchers have the species on life support, trying to help the beach-going insect move to higher ground.

Name: 29.04 Friends of Trees
Description:
"“Friends of Trees” is a neighborhood-based tree-planting program. Since
1989, the group has planted more than 600,000 trees in Portland and has
recently branched out (pardon the pun) to the Vancouver and Eugene
areas. This community-building program helps small teams of homeowners
to buy and plant trees to improve their neighborhoods. The program also
works with local agencies to restore natural areas, increase habitat,
and improve watersheds; Once mature, the trees have measurable,
significant impact on our region’s air and water quality. Additionally,
the program provides paid jobs for local youth through the Portland
Opportunities Industrialization Center at the Rosemary Anderson
alternative high school; these are youth who have trouble fitting into a
traditional public school curriculum. We profile neighbors from the
diverse Kenton and Portsmouth neighborhoods as well as the Rosemary
Anderson youth in our story."

Name: 29.04 Warner Canyon Skiing
Description: The kids want to show me their favorite run. To get to it, we cut across the groomed run and head into the trees. “It’s pretty easy to get lost if you don’t know where you’re going,” says Annette, my new volunteer backcountry ski guide. Skiing beside us are Annette’s middle school classmates, Levi and Reed. We glide through stands of pines, sun glinting down. As a reporter for “Oregon Field Guide,” I am spending the day skiing Lakeview’s Warner Canyon, one of Oregon’s smallest and most remote ski areas. Cameraman Greg Davis and I are here to experience the second-oldest ski hill in the state (started 1938 — the same year as Timberline.)

Name: 29.05 Oregon Desert Trail
Description: Sage Clegg folded her body against a tower of basalt and turned the GoPro on herself. The shade was a small defense against the blistering heat in the depth of Oregon’s Owyhee Canyonlands. “I’m definitely on edge today. Not only have I almost stepped on nine rattlesnakes, it’s also just really stressful travel,” Clegg said, tilting the camera down to her legs and revealing a field of flame-red prick marks rising in response to a brush with stinging nettle. “I really wish I wasn’t alone right now.” Clegg’s nearly 800-mile trek on the Oregon Desert Trail in 2013 took its toll, but by the end of that summer, she had become the first person to complete the entire route. Her pioneering effort put Oregon’s newest long-distance trail on the map. And yet, today, only a small handful of others have successfully managed to repeat her accomplishment.

Name: 29.05 Pyrosomes
Description: A sea creature normally found in the tropics has suddenly exploded in the ocean off the Oregon coast. Scientists are baffled by this massive bloom of pickle-shaped pyrosomes that are clogging the nets of researchers and fishermen. No one knows exactly why they’re here, but many suspect it has something to do with a blob of warm water that’s been hanging out off the West Coast for the past several years. Researchers are studying the ocean conditions to learn more about their habitat and running experiments to figure out what they’re eating.

Name: 29.05 Willamette Falls
Description: Oregon is famous for its scenic waterfalls. In fact, its tallest waterfall, Multnomah Falls, is the most visited natural recreation site in the state, attracting more than 2 million sightseers a year. Yet, the largest waterfall in Oregon is perhaps its least visited. Even though it’s just a few miles south of Portland, it’s hardly a tourist destination. That’s because for more than 150 years, Willamette Falls has been surrounded by massive industrial buildings, blocking it from public access. People who get a glimpse of the falls see it from the far cliffs of Oregon City. Few get close enough to it to feel the spray and hear the thunder of the water, which plummets from basalt cliffs four-stories tall. It’s the second largest waterfall in the U.S. by volume — the Niagara of the West.

Name: 29.06 Big River Tug
Description: We hop abroad a tug in Clarkston, Washington for the 360-mile trip to the Port of Vancouver to see what it takes to navigate huge barges through locks and underwater channels of the Snake and Columbia Rivers. Two-man crews work 24-7 for 15 straight days to keep the Northwest's agricultural and forest products circulating around the world and help keep our local economy afloat.

Name: 29.06 Horse Logger
Description: We spend a day in the woods with Oregon's last horse logger, who has returned to a family forest that he has been logging for more than 40 years. We learn that this land once belonged to a pioneer of sustainable forestry, Wendell Harmon, and now his son is carrying on his legacy.

Name: 29.06 Train Mountain Part 2
Description: It’s raining at Train Mountain, which makes the tracks too slippery to run trains. It’s also too cold to go out and rake the pine needles and cones off the tracks, still being spring in the high Ponderosa pine forests north of Klamath Lake in Southern Oregon. A handful of older men dressed in railroad overalls and grease-stained Carhartt jackets sit in Central Station and sip coffee and talk of their shared passion: trains. It’s hard to say exactly what spell trains cast on kids. For some, it is a spell never broken. For those who grow up, but not out of their passion for trains, there is a place they can go: Train Mountain.

Name: 29.09 Drones in Research
Description:
"Dr. Leigh Torres has a job that would be envied by many.
Torres, an assistant professor at Oregon State University, spends her
time cruising the open seas in search of gray whales. Although spotting
them off the Newport coast during their migration period isn’t that
hard, getting to know these whales better has been difficult in
the past.
Drones are now changing that picture.
“Through the drone footage we can follow them underwater in a completely non-invasive way,” Torres said.
And it isn’t just at sea. Oregon State University launched over 400
drone missions last year for scientific research. The drone lab takes
off-the-shelf consumer drones and arms them with high-tech sensors,
custom parts and optical equipment built to spec for research in
forestry, wildlife, agriculture and even search and rescue research.
And although drones are just one more tool in the toolbox for
science, field research without drones may one day feel as strange as a
pocket without a cell phone."

Name: 29.09 Eelgrass and Oysters
Description: Oyster farming is a more than $100 million dollar industry in Oregon and Washington. But concerns over the impact of ocean acidification on oyster production has many in the industry worried about the future. Scientists on the West Coast are exploring a possible solution – eel grass. When eel grass in the shallows photosynthesize, they take up carbon dioxide out of water, raising the PH. Could planting eel grass in proximity to commercial oyster operations mitigate acidification, and help the broader estuarine ecosystems in the process?

Name: 29.09 The Shire
Description: We head to the Shire, a 78-acre stretch of the Columbia River, directly across from Multnomah Falls. It has one of the best views in the entire Gorge Scenic Area, and that's no accident. It was the intention of architect John Yeon. We meet Yeon's life partner, Yeon author, and folks who picnicked with Yeon at the Shire, leading to the creation of the Columbia River Gorge Scenic Area.

We follow a group of scientists on a years-long monitoring project to see if the fisher is successfully taking to its new home.

Since early 2017, it's also involved a team of falconers and Harris hawks.
